blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: daughter of the bear-king! He was charmed. The girl began singing a lullaby to the following effect :—

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: ‘Prasen was killed by the lion, the lion was killed by Jāmbuvant. Do not cry, dear child, the gem is now yours.’

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: The girl saw Krishna. She fell in love at first sight and said :—

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: ‘Do leave the cave at once, before my father Jāmbuvant awakes. Take the gem, but run away?’ Krishna smiled but, instead of following her advice, blew upon his conch shell—the challenge signal!

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Jāmbuvant came forth and began the battle. It was a terrible fight!

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Krishna’s attendants watched at the door of the cave for six days, and then, thinking that he was killed by the bear, returned to Dwarka.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: The obsequies of Krishna were duly performed!

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: On the other hand, Krishna fought the battle for twenty-one days and defeated the king of the bears. The latter was pleased with Krishna. He then recollected that he was not fighting with an ordinary mortal! His adversary was God himself.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Jāmbuvant said :—‘O God, I am invincible to all the gods, demons, demi-gods, and men, and yet you have defeated me! You must therefore be the God of Gods. You are Vishnu. I know it. How could you otherwise possess such prowess? I offer you this gem along with my daughter Jāmbuvanti. Krishna was married to the princess and returned to his capital with his prizes. He informed the citizens of what happened. He then held a general meeting and in that open durbar he handed over to Satrājit the lost gem. In order to remove the ill-feeling that would arise, Satrājit also offered his daughter Satyabhāma to Krishna.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Poor Satrājit! The possession of the gem was his misfortune! One Shatdhanva and one Akrur coveted it
